category: New Tech; New Tech; Value Perception
parameter: Charging Speed; Charging Infrastructure; Ride-hailing Suitability
content_english: This review probably comes from someone who has owned an electric car as their main vehicle, which is why they have such a review. Over a year ago, I bought an e34 and sold my Ertiga because my house had a 10m frontage, so I set up home charging right away (the reviewer mentioned buying an 11kW charger without knowing that residential power can only support a maximum of 7.5kW direct connection, and the wiring from the meter to the house; anything above 7.5kW requires three-phase power, which very few homes have for residential use). Two conditions to install a 7.5kW charger: you need a parking spot inside the house plus a direct connection right at the meter. Meeting both conditions in central Saigon or Hanoi? Probably no one would buy it. I ran into trouble when I moved to District 7—I couldn't park the car inside because the road was wide, but the curb was deliberately built 3 steps high, so no car could enter the house to charge. That's when I thought about using public charging stations, but there aren't many in District 7, making it very inconvenient when I needed to charge. In the end, I sold the car at a loss. As for BYD, there's only one YouTuber who told the truth: when driving to Can Tho, a third-party charger only delivers 5kW, and with this battery, it takes over 10 hours to fully charge—note that the largest city in the Mekong Delta only has a few charging stations. And with VinFast's charging network, even in District 7, Saigon, a year ago, I saw very few spots, let alone BYD's third-party chargers, which are slow (5-7kW is considered slow charging). For an ordinary person like me, I can't even tolerate that, let alone buying one for ride-hailing. Ride-hailing drivers rely on their time for profit, but with such long charging times, how can they make it work? This car is suited for suburban drivers with spacious homes, where they can park inside, install a 7.5kW home charger, charge overnight, and drive in the morning—but they can't drive intercity because the risk of running out of battery is very high; there are no charging stations in rural areas, so even though the battery gauge shows how much is left, you can end up stuck with no nearby charger. That's just a dead end. All EV owners dread the charging process; only those who've never owned an electric car don't understand. So anyone wanting to buy one for ride-hailing should think carefully before purchasing, or else you'll end up stuck—can't keep it, can't sell it either. It's a huge loss. I sold my e34 to a guy who bought it for ride-hailing, and from talking to him, I know well that buying on installment to pay off debt is a huge pressure, supporting a family and paying off the car loan. After over a year, I think he's probably given up, but with this m6, I'm not sure.

content_english: I also run a ride-hailing service with my VF6. I once broke into a cold sweat because I ran the car down to 0% battery. The situation is quite similar to what your friend shared. From Nhon Trach to Ho Chi Minh City. 4km before getting on the Cat Lai ferry, the battery dropped to 0%, and while on the ferry, I wondered if turning off the car at 0% would recover a bit of battery or if it wouldn't start again, so I decided to keep the car running (not turning it off) until the intersection of Dong Van Cong - Nguyen Thi Dinh. At that point, the battery was at 0%, but the car only allowed a speed of 16km/h, and the air conditioning turned off automatically. I continued moving at a snail's pace (16km/h) to the charging station another 2.7km away. Yet the car made it without stalling, battery still at 0% when I reached the charging station, and it charged normally. A risky and hair-raising experience. The VF6's battery is very okay (I estimate that at 0% battery, the car can still travel another 8km based on my feeling).

content_english: I also run a ride-hailing service with my VF6. I once broke out in a cold sweat when the battery hit 0%. The situation was pretty similar to what your friend shared. I was going from Nhơn Trạch to Ho Chi Minh City. 4km before getting on the Cát Lái ferry, the battery dropped to 0%. While on the ferry, I wondered if turning off the car at 0% would recover a bit of charge or if it wouldn’t start again. I decided to keep the car running (not turn it off) all the way to the intersection of Đồng Văn Cống and Ng Thị Định. By then, the battery was at 0%, but the car only allowed me to go 16km/h. The AC turned off by itself, and I continued moving at turtle speed (16km/h) to the charging station, which was another 2.7km away. Believe it or not, the car made it without shutting down. The battery was still at 0% when I reached the charger, and it charged normally. A nerve-wracking and thrilling experience. The VF6’s battery is really impressive—I estimate that at 0%, it could still go another 8km based on how it felt.

content_original: Chuy?n t?t v? 0% r?i mà v?n ?i ???c thêm là chuy?n bình th??ng ?, ?i thêm ?c nhi?u hay ít ph? thu?c vào hãng thi?t l?p h
content_english: That's a good feature, but being able to drive further even after hitting 0% is actually normal. How much extra you can go depends on how the manufacturer sets up the BMS and power chain. The root cause is that the battery's charge/discharge curve is non-linear, with the most variation near full charge and near empty. So manufacturers calculate battery percentage within a range that can be roughly considered linear (where capacity drops relatively evenly), meaning 100% is set at the point where the battery begins to discharge steadily, and 0% is where it stops being steady. Additionally, manufacturers may set 0% at an even earlier point as a reserve for unexpected situations, like the driver in the video experienced (7:15). In short, 0% on the display doesn't necessarily mean the battery is completely drained, and 100% doesn't necessarily mean it's fully charged.

category: Value Perception; Other; Safety & ADAS; Value Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Battery Replacement Cost; Charging Time; Vehicle Reliability; Maintenance Cost; Country of Origin Image
content_english:
The electric car looks great, the Vin car also looks great. But sorry, I still choose Japanese cars.

*7-seat electric car. On-road price including spare tire, battery protection, leather seat covers... around 750 million. Running 4 years = 320,000 km - 10,000 km for charging detours = remaining 310,000 km running as shared taxi × 6,000 VND/km fare = 1,860 million VND revenue.
- Subtract 128 million for electricity (160,000 km × 800 VND/km over 2 years after free charging ends)
- Subtract 720 million for driver salary (48 months × 15 million)
- Subtract 312 million for loan interest (48 months × 6.5 million)
- Subtract 48 million for tires (16 tires × 3 million)
- Subtract 44 million for insurance (vehicle, battery, personal)
- Subtract 70 million for maintenance and repairs + battery removal, motor disassembly, coolant replacement for battery, motor bearing replacement...
- Subtract 28 million for road tolls and car washes
- Subtract 150 million for replacing worn-out battery
Total expenses = 1,500 million.
Total revenue 1,860 mil - 1,500 mil = 360 mil profit - 360 mil depreciation = zero.

*7-seat gasoline car. On-road price 600 million.
Running 4 years, 310,000 km × 6,000 fare = 1,860 million.
- Subtract 434 million for gasoline (310,000 km × 1,400 VND/km)
- Subtract 720 million for salary (48 months × 15 million)
- Subtract 259 million for loan interest (48 months × 5.4 million)
- Subtract 24 million for tires (12 tires × 2 million)
- Subtract 25 million for vehicle and personal insurance
- Subtract 70 million for maintenance + repairs + oil change + air filter, fuel filter
- Subtract 28 million for road tolls and car washes
Total expenses 1,560 million.
Revenue 1,860 mil - expenses 1,560 mil = 300 mil profit - 300 mil depreciation = zero.

Disadvantages of electric cars: battery, electricity, and spare parts may increase in price because currently they are priced low to stimulate sales volume. The cars often pose safety risks and affect work time. Risk of expensive parts failing costing from tens to hundreds of millions like DBU, power cables, inverter, battery casing, battery cell, and the AC filter also needs to cool the battery so it's both expensive and wears out faster. Inconvenient when you have to spend 1-2 hours charging. While a gasoline car rarely breaks down. If you use proper oil, the engine can run up to 500,000 km and only costs 50 million for an overhaul, which is good profit.

Currently BYD quotes a 60kWh battery at around 250 million. While VinFast's battery price is valued at 1/3 of the original battery cost, which seems unreasonable. Is Mr. Vuong pricing the battery low to lure people (like a scam)? Because currently the batteries are still new, so pricing them low has no effect. A few years from now when the batteries fail, if they raise the price to market level, nobody will dare complain.

To be honest, for a 7-seater ride-hailing vehicle, the Vinfast Limo Green is still the best! Just the fact that it can operate on both Grab and Xanh SM already puts it ahead of the competition. The battery replacement cost for the Limo Green is only around 90-120 million VND, half the price of a BYD battery, while durability is comparable since both use LFP chemistry.

category: Brand Perception; Value Perception; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Brand Trust; Value for Money; Energy Consumption
content_english:
The concern about quality is concern based on trust. If you don't trust, don't buy—no need for fake politeness either =))

Frankly, people who buy VF cars do so because they trust the quality (who knows who has better quality, but trust is enough; time will tell). They get price discounts, charging benefits, better charging infrastructure, and they're willing to support a car brand that manufactures in Vietnam. You guys criticize, but the cars you buy—how many units do they sell a year in Vietnam? No one knows. People who praise it buy it, and it sells around a thousand units every month.

Instead of sweating over finding charging stations, instead of spending 200k a day (6 million a month), they take it easy and save 6 million. Got it? And 6 million is nothing—I've seen guys who fill up twice a day, spending 8-9 million a month on gas. Drive for one year, you save an extra 70-100 million.

I drive a VF5, I signed up with GSM to drive extra hours, about 2,000 km a month, earn 7-8 million, free charging for 3.5 years until the end of 2027 (I got a 0.5 year bonus for being an early battery buyer). The car is crappy, ugly, stiff, heavy, decent tech, never spent a single day in the shop.

category: Brand Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; Other; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Other; New Tech; Other
parameter: Brand Trust; Incentives; Cost Savings; Warranty; Earning Potential; Reliability; Exterior Design; Ride Quality; General Quality; General Technology; Weight
content_english:
Caring about quality means caring based on trust. If you don’t trust, don’t buy—no need for fake politeness either =))

Frankly, people buy VF cars because they trust the quality (I don’t know who has better quality, but trusting is enough; time will tell). They get price discounts, charging benefits, better battery support, and they’re also willing to support a car brand that manufactures in Vietnam. You guys criticize, but those old cars only sell a few hundred units per year in Vietnam—nobody knows them. The people who praise them actually buy them, selling several thousand units every month.

Instead of sweating to earn a hundred bucks, instead of spending 200k/day (6 million/month) on gas, they’re relaxed and save 6 million. OK? And 6 million is nothing—I’ve even met guys who charge twice a day, spending 8-9 million a month on gas. After one year, they save an extra 70-100 million.

I drive a VF5, plug it into GSM to run extra hours on the side, drive nearly 2,000 km a month, earn 7-8 million, and get free charging for 3.5 years until the end of 2027 (I have an extra 0.5 year early adopter bonus for buying the battery). The car is crappy, ugly, stiff, heavy, and outdated tech-wise, but it hasn’t spent a single day in the shop.

content_english: Relying on private charging stations to develop is extremely difficult. Because for a high-power station (60kW and up), the minimum investment cost is 500 million. Don't be fooled by Vin's advertised price of 250 million for a 60kW station as the final price—the actual construction cost is the real kicker. You driving the M6 in the article can stick to a fixed route like that, but the key factor is that "BYD SELLS TOO FEW CARS." That's why you can always find a spot to charge when you arrive at a station. If there were many charging stations, when you can't charge at one, you could go to another. But if there's only one station in the area and it's completely full, then your travel plans are ruined—especially if you're on a passenger pick-up route.

content_english: Many Vietnamese guys have tested the Limo Green, and although it's advertised at around 450 km, they've actually gotten more, even over 500 km. BYD claims 420 km, and the driver said after about 80,000 km it still hit that range, but can it go beyond that? Vinfast falls short on technology, but makes up for it with much better real-world efficiency — simply put, the VF5 is rated for 320 km, but in reality it goes much further, some people even reaching 400 km. So isn't the comparison above a bit skewed? If Vietnamese people don't support Vietnamese products, who will? Why make a video like this out of nowhere? Why not just slap them in the face with what Vinfast actually does better? The Limo is cheaper and comes with free charging, so factoring that in against the small price gap with the BYD, isn't that a no-brainer?

parameter: Country of Origin Image; Price; Technology Features; Competitor Comparison; Dealer Network
content_english: Chinese cars entering the Vietnamese market this time have a long-term strategy: Chery, Geely, and Lynk & Co have already built factories in Vietnam, while BYD is about to launch a strategic PHEV model, the Sealion 6, imported from Thailand. It will be very competitively priced due to lower tariffs compared to importing from China like the other brands. Chinese cars offer high technology, generous features, and decent build quality, but in the Vietnamese market, pricing is crucial (the Xforce and Xpander are examples of how price determines success). I believe Chinese PHEV models will be very viable in Vietnam in the coming period, provided these automakers set up assembly plants and a nationwide network of dealerships and service centers. Only then will competitive prices be possible, allowing them to gradually capture market share from Japanese, Korean, and European brands. Vietnamese consumers will then change their perception of Chinese cars and be ready to drop their prejudices. Japanese, Korean, and European automakers will certainly face major challenges and will have to adjust their strategies. This is also good news for Vietnamese consumers, who will have the opportunity to own better cars in terms of price and quality, without features being cut—for example, a Toyota Fortuner version costing nearly 1.37 billion VND that still requires manual folding mirrors. In short, if Chinese automakers succeed in Vietnam, consumers will be the ones to benefit the most!

content_english: A new game begins. Before, Vin covered all charging stations, but now Vin won't let me charge, I won't sell the battery.
